content:
{
  "title": "Prompt Contextualization via Dynamic Safety Anchors for Robust LLM Defense",
  "problem": "Large Language Models remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attempts that coax harmful outputs, even after safety fine-tuning. Existing defense mechanisms struggle to generalize across attack types or adapt to novel jailbreak strategies.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in critical applications, adversaries continuously develop sophisticated attacks that outpace static prompt defenses. There is an urgent need for prompting methods that adapt contextually to the prompt and conversation history, providing more robust, situation-aware safety anchoring. Such adaptive techniques could raise the cost of successful jailbreaks in real-world settings.",
  "method": "We propose Dynamic Safety Anchors (DSA): an automated secondary prompt generator that, at inference time, analyzes each user prompt and recent conversation history using a lightweight classifier and retrieval system. DSA injects contextually-relevant, highly-targeted safety and refusal anchors directly into the system prompt—tailored both semantically and stylistically to the input. This dynamic anchoring disrupts known attack chains and adapts to new evasion methods, differing from static, uniform safety prompts. The DSA operates as a pre-processing layer and can be used with any frozen LLM.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on JailbreakBench, AdvBench, and a new suite of zero-day jailbreak and adversarial prompt datasets. Baselines include static safety prompting, adaptive shield prompting (e.g., AdaShield), and RLHF-only models. Metrics will measure attack success rate, benign utility retention, and anchor injection overhead."
}
